title: Will the nursing ratio trigger a wave of bed closures?
blurb: Taiwan's leading media outlet quotes Karen Lasater on the U.S. experience with nurse-to-patient ratios, noting California has not closed beds in 20 years of mandated ratios. CHOPR research is cited throughout.

title: Nurse.org covers CHOPR's Pennsylvania staffing and mortality study
blurb: Coverage of the Medical Care study finding that each additional patient added to a nurse's workload is associated with an 8% increase in the odds of 30-day patient mortality.

title: Nurses in B.C. vote overwhelmingly in favour of a strike
blurb: Karen Lasater joined multiple CBC Radio programs after British Columbia nurses voted to strike, discussing her 2025 study of nurse-to-patient ratios in BC hospitals.

title: Pennsylvania safe staffing study draws wide coverage
source: Philly Voice, Pittsburgh Post-Gazette, Becker's & others
blurb: The Medical Care study on safe minimum nurse staffing requirements in Pennsylvania was covered by Philly Voice, the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette, Becker's, and ScienMag.

title: A Safe Staffing Policy for Pennsylvania Could Prevent Deaths and Produce Savings to Help Fund Improved Staffing, New Study Finds
blurb: A CHOPR study of more than 547,000 patients across 132 Pennsylvania hospitals projects that safe-staffing ratios could prevent up to 3,040 deaths a year while generating roughly $305 million in savings.

title: Are Your Staffing Metrics Enough? New Research on Patient Falls Says Maybe Not
blurb: Analyzing more than 1,200 nursing units nationwide, this study finds nurses' own assessments of staffing adequacy predict patient falls better than traditional administrative staffing metrics.

title: Caribbean Nursing Challenges: Top Health Officials Call for Urgent Regional Action to Stabilize Workforce
blurb: Chief Nursing Officers from nineteen Caribbean countries describe chronic understaffing, workplace violence, and stifled professional development driving nurse migration — and catalog 52 policy actions in response.

title: Three Canadian Provinces are on Board with Minimum Nurse-to-Patient Ratios. Here's How Implementation is Going.
blurb: Karen Lasater is quoted — and CHOPR is mentioned — in this look at how minimum nurse-to-patient ratio implementation is going across three Canadian provinces.

title: Reattracting Oncology Nurses to Hospital Employment: Researcher Shares Organizational Insights
blurb: Two Q&A features on the JAMA Network Open reattraction study — one in Cancer Nursing Today and one in DocWireNews on fixing the nursing shortage.

title: Post-COVID, nurses still 'lack confidence in management to act in the best interest of patients'
blurb: Health Exec covers the Medical Care study on nurses' confidence in management; MedPage Today links nurse understaffing to higher risk of death and readmissions.

title: Penn Nursing Professor Named 2026 ANA President's Award Recipient
blurb: The American Nurses Association named Linda Aiken, CHOPR Founding Director, a 2026 ANA President's Award recipient for research linking nurse staffing, education, and patient safety.

title: Nursing Strikes are Making Headlines; Can Picket Lines Improve Patient Care?
blurb: Linda Aiken is quoted — and CHOPR is mentioned — in this Medscape examination of whether nursing strikes can improve patient care.

title: Staffing, Scheduling, and Wage Changes Could Reattract Nurses Back to Hospitals
blurb: HealthDay's story on the JAMA Network Open reattraction findings was picked up by Clinical Advisor, Drugs.com, and Medical Professionals Reference.

title: Magnet hospital model success in Europe
blurb: Magnet4Europe outcomes were the headline lead article in the Nursing Times (UK). A companion research update on the trial was published by the Leonard Davis Institute.

title: Broad coverage of "What Would Bring Nurses Back?"
source: MedPage Today, KFF Health News, Becker's & others
blurb: The JAMA Network Open reattraction study drew coverage from at least eight outlets, including KFF Health News, Becker's, and MedicalXpress.

title: What Would Bring Nurses Back? New Research Shows How Hospitals Can Reverse Nursing Workforce Losses
blurb: A study of 4,043 RNs who left hospital direct care finds most are motivated to return — and safe nurse staffing levels top the list of factors that would bring them back. By Karen Lasater, Matthew McHugh, and Jane Muir.

title: Virtual nursing programs get mixed reviews in Penn Nursing study
blurb: Karen Lasater is quoted in the Inquirer's coverage of the JAMA Network Open study finding virtual nursing programs get mixed reviews from bedside nurses.

title: The Real Reason Behind New York's Nurses Strike
blurb: Linda Aiken's op-ed argues the NYC nurses strike reflects patient safety risks from chronic understaffing rather than a pay dispute, drawing on two decades of CHOPR research.

title: Live TV interview on the New York City nurse strike
blurb: Linda Aiken discussed the NYC nurse strike live on NewsNation, highlighting Penn Nursing and CHOPR research on staffing and patient outcomes.

title: Landmark Magnet4Europe Intervention Improves Clinician Wellbeing and Patient Safety Across Europe
blurb: The Magnet4Europe trial, co-led by CHOPR and KU Leuven, twinned 56 European hospitals with 65 U.S. Magnet hospitals and found significant drops in nurse burnout, intent to leave, and unfavorable safety ratings.

title: Virtual nursing draws mixed reviews from bedside nurses: 5 study findings
blurb: The virtual nursing study led ANA SmartBrief and drew articles from Becker's and MedicalXpress, with pickup by MSN.

title: Penn Nursing Study: Virtual Nursing Programs in Hospitals Fall Short of Expectations
blurb: In one of the largest studies of virtual nursing to date, 57% of 880 hospital nurses surveyed said virtual nursing programs do not reduce their workload, urging caution before substituting virtual for in-person nursing.

title: Want a Happier Hospital? Hire More Nurses, Study Says
blurb: HealthDay's article on the JAMA Network Open physician burnout findings was picked up by US News & World Report and Yahoo News.

title: Investing in Nurses Reduces Physician Burnout, International Study Finds
blurb: A landmark international study finds hospitals with better nurse staffing and work environments are significantly associated with less physician burnout and job dissatisfaction.

title: Joint Commission Includes Nurse Staffing in Updated Hospital Performance Goals
blurb: Karen Lasater is quoted in coverage of the Joint Commission adding nurse staffing to hospital performance goals, referencing a 2023 CHOPR study in Nursing Outlook.

title: Penn Nursing PhD Student Selected as RWJF Health Policy Research Scholar
blurb: CHOPR fellow Miller Celestin was selected as a Robert Wood Johnson Foundation Health Policy Research Scholar; his research focuses on eliminating disparities in emergency department outcomes.

title: Facing burnout, stress and shortages, Pa. nurses push to limit patient workload
blurb: TribLive's report on the Pennsylvania safe staffing push references Linda Aiken and Penn Nursing/CHOPR research.

title: National Award & Appointment for Penn Nursing Postdoctoral Research Fellow
blurb: CHOPR postdoctoral fellow David (Hyunmin) Yu was named a STAT Wunderkind and appointed to the American Nurses Enterprise Research Advisory Council.

title: Practice Environment Scale Receives National Endorsement
blurb: The PES-5, derived from Eileen Lake's Practice Environment Scale of the Nursing Work Index, was endorsed as a national healthcare quality measure by the Partnership for Quality Measurement.

title: Partnership With Penn Nursing Expands AACN's Research on Nurse Work Environments
blurb: The American Association of Critical-Care Nurses is partnering with CHOPR on its recurring Healthy Work Environment survey, with Amanda Bettencourt and Matthew McHugh serving as researchers.

title: American Nurse Foundation Credentialing Grant for Penn Nursing Researchers
blurb: Matthew McHugh and David Yu were awarded a $150,000 Margretta Madden Styles Credentialing Research Grant to evaluate whether the Pathway to Excellence credential improves nurse and patient outcomes.

title: International Collaboration on Nursing and Midwifery in the Caribbean Deemed a Success, According to New Study
blurb: A WHO/PAHO Collaborating Center partnership led by Eileen Lake fielded a comprehensive nursing and midwifery workforce survey across 20 Caribbean countries with a 100% response rate from Chief Nursing Officers.

title: New Study: Significant Racial Discordance Between Nurses and Patients in Black-Serving Hospitals
blurb: Evaluating patient-nurse racial concordance across 574 hospitals, the study finds the nursing workforce lacks sufficient diversity, especially in Black-serving hospitals.

title: Pa.-based doctors, nurses and physical therapists will soon be able to work in dozens of other states with ease
blurb: Matthew McHugh is quoted — and Penn Nursing and CHOPR are mentioned — in this piece on Pennsylvania's implementation of multistate healthcare licensure compacts.

title: Expanding Essential Wound Care for People Who Use Drugs
blurb: A mixed-methods study in the Philadelphia region identifies strategies for expanding low-barrier wound care services for people who use drugs, timely amid the rise of xylazine-related wounds.

title: Trump budget proposes killing nursing research institute
blurb: Matthew McHugh, Martha Curley, and Mary Naylor are quoted in Science on the proposed elimination of the National Institute of Nursing Research; Penn Nursing and CHOPR are mentioned.

title: Penn Nursing Professor Wins National Research Award
blurb: K. Jane Muir won the 2025 Nurse Researcher Award from the Emergency Nurses Association for significant contributions to emergency nursing research.

title: Nurses Speak Out: Fixing Care for Disadvantaged Patients
blurb: A CHOPR study sheds light on the critical factors that help or hinder hospital nurses in providing quality care to socially disadvantaged populations, drawing on nurses' own accounts.

title: The National Institute of Nursing Research Is in Jeopardy
blurb: A MedPage Today opinion piece on threats to NINR highlights Linda Aiken's research as an example of why the institute is needed.
